- Are you Swiss?

- I am good, thank you.
- And you?
- Also good, thank you.

- Oh, moie Marc.
- Gudde Mëtteg, Madame Kaiser.
- Wéi geet et Iech?
- Ganz gutt, merci. An Dir?
- Mir geet et och gutt.
- Wunnt Dir och op de Bousbierg mam Mama?
- Nee, ech wunnen net méi zu Mama. Ech wunnen lo zu Miersch. Ech sinn haut an der Stad, fir e puer Kaddoen ze kafen.
- Also, wat mëschts du dann lo? Schaffs du schonn oder studéiers du nach?
- Ech sinn Employé op enger Bank zu Esch.
- Zu Esch? Dat ass awer wäit. Wéi fiers du dann op d'Aarbecht?
- Ech fuere normalerweis mam Auto, mee hautescht huelen ech oft den Zuch.
- Oh, mee bus ass du. Äddi Marc.
- Äddi, Madame Kaiser.
- Oh, good morning Marc.
- Good afternoon, Madam Kaiser.
- How are you?
- Very well, thank you. And you?
- I am doing well too.
- Do you also live on the Bousbierg with your mother?
- No, I no longer live with mom. I now live in Miersch. I am currently in town to buy a few gifts.
- So, what are you doing now then? Are you already working or still studying?
- I am employed at a bank in Esch.
- In Esch? That is quite far. How do you get to work then?
- I usually drive by car, but today I often take the train.
- Oh, but you take the bus. Bye Marc.
- Bye, Madam Kaiser.

- Allô, hajj aschaja?
- Gudde Moien, mäi Numm ass Claudine Hauselma.
- Esch rufen unn wengs era annons, as Platz nach frei?
- Jo, huddir schon Erfahrung mad Kanner?
- Jo, esch hoffen am Summer als Monitoris a Kalonien, an esch studiere Pädagogie.
- Ah, flott! A wie alt siedir dann?
- Esch hun 19 Joa.
- Esch hun e Bu vun drei Joa.
- Esch isch ne Babysitter fir op mannst zwei Molwoch.
- Wenni genau?
- Normalerweis Dönnesdes an Donnerschdesowes, so sechs bis neng, mi Hayams do och Duwigand.
- Hm, de Wygand? Esch wees net.
- Duwigand bezül nischt natierlisch a bisse mi?
- Esch proposeyren 10 Euro d'stonn, an Duwigand gedaus supplement vu 5 Euro d'stonn.
- Oh, dat sinn esch ofverstanden.
- Wun dir an der Staat?
- Jo, esch wunnen umlampatspiersch.
- Dat as praktisch. Esch wunnen och an der Staat.
- Da kann esch de Busch holen.
- Tip top. Kommt muerde Nuevend gengt 6 Auer Lanschd, an da schwätze mer iwer Detailer vun der Aaböschd.
- Anderrei, wie as era Dress, wann isch glift?
- Singruwigte Hugo.
- Gut, bis muer. Adi.
- Avair.
- Hello, who are you?
- Good morning, my name is Claudine Hauselma.
- I’m calling and posting a bit, is there a space available?
- Yes, do you already have experience with children?
- Yes, I hope in the summer as a monitor in colonies, and I study pedagogy.
- Oh, great! And how old are you then?
- I am 19 years old.
- I have a child of three years.
- I am a babysitter for at most two afternoons a week.
- When exactly?
- Usually Tuesday and Thursday evenings, about six to nine, with Hayams there too on Sunday.
- Hmm, the evening? I don’t know.
- Sunday evenings are not really natural and a bit with me?
- I offer 10 euros per hour, and Sunday evenings a supplement of 5 euros per hour.
- Oh, that is clear.
- Where are you staying in the state?
- Yes, I live near Lampersche?
- That’s practical. I also live in the state.
- Then I can take the bus.
- Great. Come Wednesday around 6 o’clock outer country, and we’ll talk about the details of the job.
- Otherwise, how is the dress, when is it possible?
- Wednesday Hugo.
- Okay, see you tomorrow. Bye.
- Avair.

- Ech si Managerin op enger Bank an ech maache vill Geschäftsreezen.
- Vum Findel aus hunn ech gutt Verbindungen op Berlin, op London an op Genf.
- Fir op Paräis an op Bréissel huelen ech den Zuch.
- Mam Zuch kommen ech direkt an den Zentrum.
- Dat ass praktesch.
- Mat dem TGV fir op Paräis ass daier,
- an den Zuch fir op Bréissel ass lues.
- I am a manager at a bank and I handle many business trips.
- From Findel I have good connections to Berlin, to London, and to Geneva.
- To Paris and to Brussels I take the train.
- By train I arrive directly at the center.
- That is practical.
- With the TGV to Paris it is expensive,
- and the train to Brussels is cheap.

- Ech si Proff.
- Ech fueren oft mat dem Auto op d'Schaff.
- Ech fuere mam Vëlo Schaffen.
- Dat ass praktesch an ech maachen och e bësse Sport.
- De Moment ass mäi Vëlo futti.
- Dofir fueren ech mam Vëlo.
- Ech kann och mam Bus fueren, mee dat ass méi deier an dauert relativ laang.
- I am Prof.
- I often drive to the factory by car.
- I ride my bicycle to work.
- That is practical and I also do a bit of sports.
- At the moment my bicycle is broken.
- Therefore I ride by bicycle.
- I can also take the bus, but that is more expensive and takes quite a long time.

- Mir fuere reegelméisseg mam Auto op Dreibach.
- Mir brauche soss eng hallef Stonn.
- Dat ass méi séier wéi mam Zuch, mee eng Parkplaz ze fannen ass net ëmmer einfach.
- Dreibach ass eng flott Stad.
- D'Leit si frëndlech, a si verstonne souguer Lëtzebuergesch.
- An der Aalstad gëtt et vill Boutiquen, Caféen a Restauranten.
- We regularly drive by car to Dreibach.
- We also need about half an hour.
- That is faster than by train, but finding a parking space is not always easy.
- Dreibach is a nice city.
- The people are friendly, and they even understand Luxembourgish.
- In Aalstad there are many boutiques, cafés, and restaurants.

- Faya, ech wunnen zudiden Heueren, mee ech schaffen zu Lëtzebuerg.
- Ech fuere mam Auto schaffen.
- Op der Autobunn ass emmer vill Traffik, an ech stibbal al Dech am Stau.
- Dat nervt.
- Mee de Benzin zu Lëtzebuerg ass billesch, dat ass super.
- A menger Firma gedet vill Frontaljéen.
- Net just aus Frankräich, mee och aus Däitschland an aus der Belsch.
- Hello, I live near the Heueren, but I work in Luxembourg.
- I go to work by car.
- On the highway there is always a lot of traffic, and I push my head into the traffic jam.
- That’s annoying.
- But the gasoline in Luxembourg is cheap, that is great.
- In my company they hire a lot of frontaliers.
- Not only from France, but also from Germany and from Belgium.
